Decline and Fall
On February 16, 2011, large-scale demonstrations and protests erupted in Libya, with the populace demanding the resignation of the revolutionary leader Colonel Muammar Gaddafi, who had been in power since 1969 for 42 years, and calling for democratic change.
Starting from March 19, military forces from multiple countries including the The United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, France, and The United States of America launched airstrikes against Libya. Gaddafi repeatedly delivered speeches, urging his supporters to resist the rebel forces and NATO. In a phone call, Gaddafi warned former British Prime Minister Tony Blair that if he lost power, terrorist groups would rise in the Middle East and would inevitably attack Europe.
2
On August 22, Libyan anti-government forces entered the capital Tripoli, declaring they had seized control and were ruling Libya. After the fall of Tripoli, Gaddafi fled to his hometown of Sirte, constantly changing his hiding places. Mansour Daou, Gaddafi's former head of security, stated that Gaddafi, his son Mutassim Gaddafi, and over twenty close associates were almost completely isolated during their desperate escape, hiding in abandoned houses without television, telephones, or even electricity, using candles for light. Gaddafi passed the time by reading books, taking notes, or brewing tea on a charcoal stove. "He did not command the battles; his son did. He made no deployments, nor did he think of any plans."
3
"Gaddafi also felt pressure because his friends had abandoned him, including The Prime Minister of Italy Silvio Berlusconi, President of France Nicolas Sarkozy, Turkish Prime Minister Recep Tayyip Erdoğan, and former British Prime Minister Tony Blair. This disappointed him, as he had considered them to be very close friends."
Gaddafi, who had ruled a country of six million people with an iron fist, was in his final phase "tense, angry, sometimes furious, and mostly in a state of sadness and anger. Even later, when we told him Tripoli had been captured, he still believed the Libyan people loved him."
Due to raging warfare, Sirte was damaged, with power and water cut off and food scarce, causing Gaddafi to become very disappointed and unsettled. During this period, Mutassim was leading the fighting in Sirte, while Saif al-Islam Gaddafi never came here. In Sirte, Gaddafi's loyal fighters were all commanded by Mutassim. They initially numbered 350, but many later fled, leaving only around 150.
While surrounded by the opposition, others had repeatedly advised Gaddafi to surrender or leave Libya, but Gaddafi and his son Mutassim did not even consider these two options. In Sirte, Gaddafi and his close associates changed their hiding places approximately every four days, primarily hiding in houses abandoned by fleeing local residents.
On the day of his capture, Gaddafi and his close associates were attempting to flee Sirte by car, but their convoy was bombed by NATO. Gaddafi was wounded and subsequently captured.
Before his death, Gaddafi was believed to have left a will, requesting to be buried in the clothes he died in and to be interred in his family's cemetery in his hometown of Sirte in accordance with Islamic customs. In his will, Gaddafi also demanded that his family be treated well and called on the Libyan people to safeguard their "identity, history, achievements, and the glory of their ancestors and heroes," urging them to "continue the fight to the end against any foreign aggressor today, now, and in the future." He also stated that he had rejected "many suggestions" for the sake of "honor and responsibility," choosing instead to continue his "resistance against foreign aggressors."
Gaddafi (left) and his designated successor Mutassim Gaddafi, who were both executed in 2011
Death on the Battlefield
On October 20, 2011, forces of the Libyan National Transitional Council announced they had captured Gaddafi in Sirte. Gaddafi was apprehended in a deserted drainage pipe in his hometown of Sirte. After being subjected to abuse, he was killed by fatal successive gunshots to the head and abdomen.
After Gaddafi's death from severe injuries, his body was not immediately buried according to Islamic practice. Instead, his remains, along with those of his son Mutassim, were placed on public display in a meat cold storage facility.
26
In the early morning of October 25, the ruling Libyan authority, the National Transitional Council (hereinafter referred to as the "NTC"), buried the body of former leader Muammar Gaddafi at a secret location within Libya. His son Mutassim and a close associate of Gaddafi were also buried. To prevent his grave from becoming a "holy site" or from being deliberately vandalized, it was decided to secretly bury Gaddafi deep in the desert. The burial site would have no markers such as a headstone, and the funeral was attended only by some tribal chiefs and religious figures. Reports indicated that there were only four "witnesses" to the funeral, all of whom swore on the Quran never to reveal the location of the grave.
According to Egyptian media reports, former Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak saw the television news and footage of Gaddafi's killing in the hospital, was extremely saddened and grieved, broke into hysterical sobs, and soon lost consciousness. The report cited informed hospital sources as saying, "After Mubarak lost consciousness, if doctors had not taken timely measures and given him sedatives, his heart would have stopped beating."
Gaddafi (left) with former Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak
Aftermath
On May 2, 2012, a judicial official from Libya's National Transitional Council announced that Libya had legislated a ban on praising or glorifying Gaddafi: glorifying the country's former leader Muammar Gaddafi, his regime, his ideology, or his son Mutassim Gaddafi constitutes a crime, and those who glorify them will be sentenced to prison. The judicial official also stated that if such reports, rumors, or propaganda endanger the state, the perpetrators will be sentenced to life imprisonment; furthermore, under a state of war, those who spread rumors that hinder military activities, create an atmosphere of terror, or weaken civilian morale will also be sentenced to prison.
According to Libyan law, Libya has remained in a state of war since overthrowing Gaddafi's regime in 2011.
7
On September 6, 2021, Libyan authorities indicated an intention to rebury the remains of Gaddafi himself and his son Mutassim, who were killed in 2011.

Governance Initiatives
Military
Between 1973 and 1974, France sold Libya nine batteries of the "Crotale" surface-to-air missile system, comprising 27 vehicles, primarily for airfield defense. During the 1970s and early 1980s, Gaddafi procured large quantities of weaponry and equipment from the Soviet Union, establishing a formidable Soviet-style air defense system. Moscow supplied Tripoli with a total of 39 S-75M3 Volga surface-to-air missile battalions, 36 S-125M surface-to-air missile battalions, and 7 Kvadrat self-propelled surface-to-air missile regiments (140 vehicles). This was later supplemented with the supply of 2 groups comprising 4 battalions of S-200VE long-range surface-to-air missile regiments and 1 Osa-AK surface-to-air missile regiment (20 vehicles). During Gaddafi's rule, the Libyan Army also received from the Soviet Union 144 Strela-1 surface-to-air missile system vehicles. Between 1984 and 1985, it procured 60 Strela-10 surface-to-air missile system vehicles, along with a large number of 14.5mm, 23mm, 30mm, and 57mm anti-aircraft guns, including the ZSU-23-4, as well as Soviet-made portable surface-to-air missile systems such as the Strela-2M, Strela-3, and Igla-1.

By the mid-1980s, all these units and subunits were organized into 18 air defense missile brigades. These comprised 9 composite brigades of S-75M3 and S-125M systems, 7 Kvadrat brigades, 1 Osa-AK brigade, 1 S-200VE brigade, and an additional brigade equipped with the French-made "Crotale" surface-to-air missile system.

Politics
During his 42 years in power, Gaddafi employed various means to weaken potential rivals. According to Libyan law established in 1972, demonstrations and forming political parties could be punishable by any penalty, including the death penalty. Libya's traditional political elites had been largely eliminated by Gaddafi, and opponents who fled abroad were to be treated as traitors. He then restructured the political system, from the initial Revolutionary Committees to the Arab Socialist Union and later to the People's Committees. Wave after wave of "suspect" old revolutionary comrades were purged, with ultimate power concentrated solely within his own family and his most loyal confidants.

Foreign Relations
Anti-Western Colonialism
Gaddafi expelled Italians, reclaimed British and American military bases, nationalized the oil industry, launched a "Cultural Revolution" aimed at eliminating Western influence, supported terrorist actions against Western countries, and subsequently faced Western sanctions, severed diplomatic ties with Western nations, and even engaged in military conflict with the United States.
After the September 11 attacks in 2001, Gaddafi made an unusual turn, strongly condemning terrorist activities and becoming one of the earliest Arab leaders to denounce terrorism at the time. Seizing this opportunity, Libya's relations with the West improved. In 2003, Libya reached a compensation agreement with the United States and the United Kingdom for the Lockerbie bombing.

Relations with the United States
In June 1970, Gaddafi ordered the reclamation of the Wheelus Air Base in Libya from The United States of America, expelling 6,000 American military personnel.
In 1972, Gaddafi annulled nine military, economic, and technical cooperation agreements previously signed between the former King and the United States. He declared the United States to be the primary enemy of the Arab states, and diplomatic missions between Libya and the United States were downgraded from ambassadorial to charge d'affaires level.
In 1973, Gaddafi declared the entire Gulf of Sidra to be Libyan territorial waters in order to restrict the activities of U.S. vessels.
At the end of 1979, Libya supported Iran against the United States. Demonstrators burned down the U.S. Liaison Office in Libya, and both sides withdrew their diplomatic personnel from each other's country.
10
In 1993, seeking to break out of isolation, Gaddafi changed his stance and lavishly praised then-U.S. President Bill Clinton as the "savior of the new world." He subsequently announced the abandonment of weapons of mass destruction development and agreed to compensate the families of the Pan Am Flight 103 victims.
In September 2004, then-President of the United States of America George W. Bush formally terminated trade sanctions against Libya. In 2006, the United States and Libya re-established diplomatic relations.
African Policy
Gaddafi's Libya provided Africa with its first modern revolution—connecting the entire African continent, including rural areas, through the application of telephones, television, radio, and other technologies such as telemedicine and distance learning.
He established the African Investment Bank in Sirte, Libya, and in 2011 founded the African Monetary Fund with a capital of 42 billion US dollars in Yaoundé and the African Central Bank based in Abuja, the capital of Nigeria.
On May 25, 1988, on the occasion of the 25th anniversary of the founding of the Organization of African Unity, Gaddafi announced his willingness to restore diplomatic relations with eight countries: The Republic of Kenya, Gabon, Zaire (now The Democratic Republic of the Congo), Ivory Coast, Liberia, Mauritius, The Republic of Senegal, and The Republic of The Gambia, as a gift to the Organization of African Unity. In the late 1980s, State of State of State of Libya's relations with these countries gradually improved, with 14 leaders from sub-Saharan African countries visiting Libya. In August 1991, Sudanese President Omar al-Bashir visited Libya three times; in March 1992, Libya restored diplomatic relations with Liberia; in 1993, the presidents of Niger and Ghana visited Libya; in May 1994, Libya formally established diplomatic relations with South Africa. In March 1996, it provided 2 million US dollars in aid to Niger and a grant of 200,000 US dollars to Republic of Mali.

Economy
Gaddafi led this country of approximately 5 million people out of poverty. In 1981, Libya's per capita national income reached as high as 11,000 US dollars, making it the wealthiest country in Africa.
24
People's Livelihood
As of 2011, under Gaddafi's leadership, Tripoli underwent extensive municipal construction, leading to a dramatic transformation of the city's appearance. Modern highways could be seen extending in all directions, and the Mediterranean-facing waterfront boulevard was bustling with traffic yet not congested.
Under Gaddafi's policies, and with the substantial revenue generated from oil and natural gas resources, the standard of living for the Libyan people significantly improved. All citizens enjoyed free healthcare and education, and the state subsidized the prices of essential goods such as food. Many residents lived in newly constructed apartment buildings and brick houses, and most families owned a car. Gaddafi actively worked to improve housing conditions for the populace. In the capital, Tripoli, and the country's second-largest city, Benghazi, tens of thousands of units of affordable housing were built. These residences, constructed as government-subsidized housing with state funding, were available to Libyans who had started a family; they could apply to the government for housing and obtain it at a very low, even symbolic, price. In terms of telecommunications, domestic calls using landline telephones were free in Libya, and mobile phone rates were also very inexpensive. By July 2010, Libya's mobile phone penetration rate was 103%, meaning the number of mobile phone subscriptions in Libya exceeded its total population.
In 1983, the parliament under Gaddafi's control approved the final plan for the Great Man-Made River Project: to build 2,485 miles of concrete pipes over 50 years, forming a massive water conveyance system. The project was divided into five phases, with a total cost nearing $25 billion, to be borne by the Libyan government itself without foreign aid. The Great Man-Made River Project officially commenced in 1984, and by 2011, the first three phases had been completed. From 1989 to 1991, approximately 1,500 miles of pipelines channeled fresh water to three enormous reservoirs. In 1996, water reached Tripoli, and in 2007, it arrived at the northeastern town of Gharyan. By 2011, the project was capable of transporting about 6.5 million cubic meters of water daily for agricultural use and human consumption.

Assessment
Overview
Muammar Gaddafi served as the supreme leader of Libya for 42 years and was a highly controversial figure, known for his unconventional and defiant style, as well as his often contradictory stances and attitudes. Public opinion about him has been sharply divided. In the eyes of his supporters, Gaddafi was a charismatic figure who delivered impassioned and highly provocative speeches.
5
Since coming to power in 1969, Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi remained at odds with Western nations for decades. However, both his supporters and critics have had to acknowledge that Gaddafi successfully led Libya, a nation of 6 million people, to become the first in Africa to lift itself out of poverty. He provided military and economic assistance to the African National Congress in its struggle against apartheid.
During his rule, Libya's economy experienced rapid development. The ideological framework established in his The Green Book and the Third International Theory had a profound influence on Libya's development for nearly half a century.
English and Russian editions of Gaddafi's The Green Book
Notable Assessments
Ronald Bruce St John: His early life experiences made Gaddafi realize that only by fully harnessing the power of the people could Libya's revival be achieved. However, the significant resistance he encountered domestically at the beginning of his rule made him realize that only by expanding his personal power could he realize his ideals. This led Libya's political system inevitably onto a path of personal authoritarianism. But he also emphasized Gaddafi's irreplaceable role in Libya's development.
Ronald Bruce St John, *A History of Libya*: Gaddafi never lacked challengers, but after taking power, his charismatic leadership and distributive economic policies won the regime a relatively high degree of popular support. Simultaneously, he played a balancing act between three institutions—the armed forces, the People's Committee system, and the revolutionary committees—which constituted the core forces supporting the regime.
Former South African President Nelson Mandela: Gaddafi is one of the revolutionary icons of our time.
Alan Hart, biographer of Yasser Arafat: Arab nationalism gave Gaddafi a delusional notion that he had the right to interfere anywhere in the Arab world. Therefore, whenever internal issues arose in Morocco or Jordan, Gaddafi was rather annoyingly eager to voice his opinion. This gradually made him an unwelcome figure in the Arab world. Yasser Arafat, who never particularly liked Gaddafi, referred to him as the "Knight of Revolutionary Phrases."
Yin Gang (Researcher at the Institute of West Asian and African Studies, Chinese Academy of Social Sciences): Gaddafi was a utopian seeker who was not highly educated. Gaddafi was like the paramount chief of a vast tribal alliance. He expressed dissatisfaction with all the world's systems, saying that capitalism was no good, socialism was no good, and democracy was no good either; only his system of direct rule by the people was best. He managed the country for 42 years using primitive methods.
Personal Life
Personality and Preferences
On any occasion, Gaddafi always wore clothing of the same color, sometimes a white shirt, white pants, white shoes, white headscarf, and white gloves, with a white cloak with gold trim worn over it; other times, a yellow khaki military uniform with a headscarf of the same color, making his appearance under the protection of over a dozen bodyguards. Gaddafi's diet was very simple; his breakfast consisted of bread and camel milk. Lunch was often grilled beef or steak, accompanied by Libyan soup, and sometimes he also ate couscous, beloved by the Berbers. He loved football and was a shareholder in the historic Italian football club Juventus F.C.

Gaddafi wearing a white cloak with gold trim over it
Legendary Marriage
There are various accounts of how Gaddafi met his second wife, with the most legendary version being that he married her precisely because she was the person who attempted to assassinate him.
At the celebration marking the first anniversary of the founding of The Great Socialist People's Libyan Arab Jamahiriya in 1970, Gaddafi was happily reviewing the troops on the podium. At that moment, a female nurse responsible for first aid quietly walked onto the podium and quickly approached Gaddafi. Taking advantage of his inattention, she suddenly opened the first aid kit, grabbed the pistol inside, and pointed it at Gaddafi's head. Just as she was about to pull the trigger, Gaddafi happened to turn his head and saw her. The 28-year-old Gaddafi, faced with the dark muzzle of the gun and the young woman before it, was at a loss. Meanwhile, the nurse, her eyes wide open as she looked at the handsome Gaddafi, momentarily forgot that she was an assassin sent to kill him and slowly lowered her gun. At this, Gaddafi smiled. As he was about to step forward, his guards, who had just reacted, rushed over and subdued the female assassin. Gaddafi ordered the guards to step back and then learned that the woman's name was Safia. One week later, Gaddafi and Safia held their wedding. Later, Safia trained a highly skilled all-female military unit for her husband.
This sounds incredible, and indeed, the truth is actually like this: In 1969, Gaddafi was on an inspection tour outside the capital when a sudden intense feeling of discomfort forced him to stop work and go to a local hospital for a check-up. The doctor on duty said it was just a common cold, but a female nurse present disagreed with the doctor, insisting it was appendicitis. This female nurse was Safia Gaddafi. Later examinations proved Safia was correct. This chance encounter led to the marriage of Gaddafi and Safia.

Eccentric Temperament
In 1988, Gaddafi personally drove a bulldozer to demolish the walls of a prison in Tripoli, releasing 400 political prisoners. Gaddafi was anti-American, yet he played a very significant role in securing the release of Western hostages held in The Lebanese Republic and Republic of the Philippines. Incognito Inspections
In 1971, Gaddafi made surprise visits to some government offices and found many staff chatting over coffee, which greatly displeased him. To ensure they focused their main energy on work, he sent military trucks to the government office buildings the very next day to remove most of the furniture from the offices. On another occasion, he disguised himself as a commoner and told a doctor his father was seriously ill. The doctor, eager to get off work, simply gave him two Aspirin tablets. Adherence to His Dream
In his early years, Gaddafi was an avid admirer of Gamal Abdel Nasser, a fact he himself made no secret of. Whether in the objectives he set for the "September Revolution" or in his speeches during the "September Revolution," he clearly emulated the revolution Nasser had launched in Egypt two decades earlier.

Controversies
Personal Austerity
Gaddafi was a devout Muslim. After coming to power, he advocated for the restoration of the "purity" of Islam in Libya. Libya was originally an exporter of alcoholic beverages, but Gaddafi issued decrees prohibiting the brewing and sale of all hard liquor.
Gaddafi came from a nomadic ethnic background and advocated for the natural equity of tribal society. He opposed extravagance and luxury, leading a simple life himself. His personal life was very austere; he only liked to drink mineral water and camel milk. He preferred living in tents over luxurious mansions and enjoyed riding camels rather than riding in luxury cars. His wife and children lived in military barracks in Tripoli. He conducted official business and received foreign guests in his tent. In 1989, when he attended the Non-Aligned Summit in Yugoslavia, he stayed in a tent he brought with him.
15
The Iron Man's Tender Side: Gaddafi enjoys the simple pleasure of playing with his grandchildren in his tent
Family Extravagance
While many firmly believed Gaddafi led a simple life, opposing voices argued that he lived a life of luxury, also presenting evidence to support their claims.
In August 2011, when Libyan opposition forces stormed the capital, residences of Gaddafi's family in Tripoli were seized by the opposition. Multiple exposed luxury mansions revealed the opulent lifestyle once enjoyed by Gaddafi's family. The resort compound featured a diving center, tennis courts, swimming pools, a football field, restaurants, and a hospital. Each of Gaddafi's children had their own independent villa; some villas even had private gyms and swimming pools, with a row of jet skis moored by the sea. Gaddafi's bedroom was very spacious, furnished with exquisite furniture.
Opposition forces also found traces of Gaddafi's luxurious life within the Bab al-Aziziya military barracks in the capital, Tripoli. In addition to lavishly furnished living quarters, there were also a massive gym, a golf course, and a helicopter landing pad, among other facilities. Gaddafi directly controlled over 140 tons of gold reserves held by the Central Bank of Libya, which amounted to nearly 8 billion US dollars in cash value.
